CHAPTER 1
THE PROBLEM AND ITS BACKGROUND
This chapter serves as the foundation for the study topic, providing necessary context and justification. It summarizes the study’s aims and emphasizes its relevance in scholarly debate. Furthermore, it gives a succinct outline of the study’s scope and any limits, establishing the groundwork for understanding the research challenge and strategy.
Introduction
There is a large market demand for Animation as a whole, both for large studios to pump out high-end content on monthly schedules and independent creators striving to tell their own unique stories. (Yahoo! Finance, 2025: 6Wresearch, 2025). The reality of animation is that it is an inherently complex and labor-intensive work process, as animators are driven to meet this growing demand, they may face major studios pushing them to their limit in order to catch up to growing production deadlines, while simultaneously indie animators face a formidable barrier to entry (Finlay, 2026; Geiger, 2025). From this demand emerges a human cost that increasingly grows more and more prevalent, such as Japanese animators being crushed under the scheduling and demands of studios like MAPPA, while systemic exploitation persists despite flying global revenue (Animehunch, 2025). Emerging markets like India face their own issues, wherein consumption and support for the industry is rapidly growing but homegrown artists are kept away from opportunities due to insufficient training infrastructure or barriers hidden in the structure of their industry (Das, 2025).
The emergence of Generative AI has led many to propose and present it as a technological solution to these systemic issues, wherein Artificial Intelligence is used as a tool for automation of tasks such as in-betweening and colorization across the animation pipeline (Tang et al, 2025). Unfortunately, many proposed implementations of AI in the mainstream have exacerbated these problems, or made new ones in the industry, by appropriating the works of artists who do not consent to it, or present AI as a means of reducing the workforce and displacing actual human animators, instead of creative empowerment. An AI application that is made with respect to artists and art must be made with their best interests in mind, that the technology should be serving and empowering animators with full creative control of the process.
This thesis documents the development and evaluation of CART (Colorization AI with Reference Transfer), software designed to automate the repetitive task of coloring animation frames in secret, using the artist’s own work as a reference, replicating that color scheme across uncolored frames, preserving creative authority and control. CART will develop upon recent advances in reference-based colorization transformers and scalable AI pipelines that prioritize artist input (Suzuki et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025), CART is developed with three core commitments: Respecting intellectual property of users, seamless integration with existing animation workflows and respect to artist’s authority. Animators will be given the means to delegate color application to the machine, which will color the frames for them and solely that, empowering the artist to make more creative decisions, all in their hands.
Background of the study
The choice to use AI as specifically a system for automated animation colorization stems from the inherently demanding nature of 2d animation production. In a traditional cel-animation pipeline, the process of coloring each individual frame requires animators to attend to hundreds of drawing while maintaining strict visual consistency all across the sequence (Tang et al., 2025; Finlay, 2026). This labor is just one of many critical responsibilities in the process, among them are storyboarding, layout designs, keyframe animation, inbetweening, compositing and post-production work. The nature of 2d animation demands manual effort and technical expertise (Tang et al., 2025). The Japanese animation market alone reached 60 billion USD in 2023 while production remains dependent on manual labor (Suzuki et al., 2025). Throughout all of animation history, production efficiency and artistic quality has been interlinked, Disney’s Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs required over 200,00 hand-drawn frames, and contemporary studios struggle with tightening production timelines (Tang et al., 2025: Geiger, 2025)
In our goal to empower animators to have more time without sacrificing creative control, we have selected specifically to automate the coloring process for two reasons. First, colorization is one of the most repetitive and technically demanding stage of the animation pipeline, a tedium where work continues until days before broadcast (Animehunch, 2024). Through industry analysis it was determined that character animation and detailed frame-by-frame work drive costs upwards because they demand intensive labor (Finlay, 2026). The second reason is that frame coloring is inherently less creatively satisfying or intrusive to automate, color schemes and palettes are already decided in preproduction. There is no creative decisions to be made in colorization, unlike storyboarding or keyframe animation which demands choices in composition, acting and narrative from the artist. This means that by automating this task, creative control remains in the hands of the artist while giving them a means to address production bottlenecks. CART is a vision-based tool designed to colorize animation frames based on user-provided reference materials, saving time on tedious tasks while keeping creative control in the artist’s hands.
Global market demand has driven unprecedented expansion in the animation industry, projections indicate continued growth that studios struggle to match (6Wresearch, 2025; Research Nester, 2025; Yahoo! Finance, 2025). This struggle to match demand has lead to concerning patterns to emerge across the industry, as major studios have been documented pushing animators to work to their physical detriment under crushing schedules, a pertinent example being the production of Jujutsu Kaisen season 2 at MAPPA, where staff were driven to work unsustainable hours, episodes would be completed days before airing (Animehunch, 2024). These incidents are a result of systemic issue: United Nations reports have pointed out Japan’s anime industry for “excessively long working hours” and in comparison low pay, which forced the hand of legislation to protect freelance workers (Animehunch, 2025). Industry data determines that animators in their early twenties earn less than 2 million yen (12,948USD) annually, which is very much below average wages in Tokyo and less than half of what the same position would earn in the United States, without formal contracts or union protections (Animehunch, 2025). Independent creators are affected by these structural pressures as well, lacking the resources and infrastructure to develop a presence in the industry, develop their brands or gather an audience while simultaneously managing every aspect of production with minimal support (Geiger, 2025; Das, 2025). In emerging markets such as India, creators often struggle against gaps in education, cultural stigma and infrastructure designed to facilitate consumption instead of content creation (Das, 2025).
CART addresses the time burden of repetitive labor, enabling artists to use their own art as reference material for the process of colorization, empowering them to delegate color application to the machine while preserving creative authority, allowing them more time to focus on higher-level creative decisions, such as character design, writing, audience engagement, etc. Artists will be empowered to have more time to exercise their human judgement and artistic vision. For independent creators with limited resources, time savings translates directly into audience building and community engagement, the direct-to-audience pipeline that determines profitability in a market where traditional financing has fallen to the wayside (Geiger, 2025). For animation studios, it offers relief from a demanding production task without compromising individual creative contribution.
Developing CART as an ethical, artist-centered AI tool that automates repetitive frame colorization tasks, allows this project to advance SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) through the digital transformation of animation production pipelines, serving as a case study of how technological innovation can strengthen creative industries while keeping workers and their needs first.
CART was conceived with a commitment to the notion that technology should serve artists instead of replacing them, of addressing bottlenecks in production without sacrificing or extracting creative labor as raw material. Animators deserve tools designed around their needs, not tools that work to their displacement. This stands in deliberate contrast to some of the more mainstream AI development that involve systematic appropriation of artist’s work without credit or compensation, and working towards workforce reduction instead of empowerment (Six, 2025; American Bar Association, 2025; Copyright Alliance, 2025). CART uses only the artist’s own work for its functioning, and intends to seamlessly integrate into any workflow, reducing time on repetitive labor while empowering creative flexibility. 
Needs/Problems Analysis
Performance
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
The demand for animation consistently outpaces the production capacity of studios and individual animators, this demand continues to grow day by day. Global consolidation and reduced greenlighting from major platforms exaggerate this issue. This makes it particularly difficult for independent animators to break into the industry or sustain a career (Roxborough et al., 2026).
Animation studios continue to struggle with under-capacity and layoffs under the weight of increasing demand. The exhausted talent pool leads to the inability to meet those demands, or unsustainable work conditions. (Hertz, 2025).
The presence of tools such as CART in the animation pipeline that allows repetitive tasks to be delegated to software instead, allowing the potential for studios to scale their production without vastly increasing human labor. This would enable consistent production quality for studios.


Information
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
Available resources in the animation industry are fragmented and limited, there is a lack of standardized and accessible resources for workflow management. (Jun & Pillai, 2024).
Studios often rely on their own manual methods and legacy systems, which can contribute to inefficiencies in production and communication, as well as difficulties in project management (Jun & Pillai, 2024).
CART enables studios to have a tool that standardizes the colorization process using their own art as reference. The tool is accessible and enables artistic consistency without overhauling established systems and workflows.


Economics
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
Animation demands a high level of expertise and thus significant capital investment, the cost of employing skilled professionals being appropriately prohibitive. Animation is a sink of time and money that in many cases, animators are not sure they will get returns from (Worstall, 2015; Roxborough et al., 2026).
Profitability is defined by a monopoly over profitable Intellectual Properties, creating an ecosystem that promotes meeting demands for established products instead of creating new ones (Worstall, 2015; Hertz, 2025).
The partial automation of the colorization process, which is tedious and labor intensive work, reduces the cost of skilled labor per project, making animation projects less risky. This gives studios more capacity to produce and market original works, and for indie animators to have access to professional automation tools, granting them less time to animate and more time to build a presence.


Control
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
The process of manual animation is inherently time consuming, to such a degree that animators and studios often prioritize production logistics over creative experimentation to make returns on their time investment. Production needs to be planned around tedious phases like in-betweening or coloring, which limits the time available for high-level creative decisionmaking (Jun & Pillai, 2024).


Directors and animators often pre-commit to color schemes, designs, style and choices early in the pipeline because later changes would involve redoing manual coloring work, limiting their personal creative flexibility once they start. This rigid workflow limits iteration and artistic risk-taking (Jun & Pillai, 2024).
Animators should have access to tools like CART, that will free them from repetitive time-consuming tasks like manual colorization, empowering them to focus on performance, emotion and storytelling. The animator retains complete control of the color palette and style, while the AI handles repetitive tasks.


Efficiency
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
The coloring of animation frames is time consuming, and often a bottleneck in production. Inefficiencies present in this phase can delay entire projects by days or weeks. (Jun & Pillai, 2024)
Manual coloring methods negatively impact production timelines, in the event of mistakes such as color inconsistencies between frames, the need for clean-up and the subsequent volume of manual work creates strong drains on resources (Jun & Pillai, 2024).
CART can streamline this workflow through consistent reference-based colorization across entire sequences. This ensures that color remains uniform throughout a project, as well as shrinking the time between rough animatic and finished product.


SERVICE
PROBLEM
CURRENT STATE
IDEAL STATE
Animation Studios tend to struggle to scale their services in high-demand environments. To meet tighter deadlines, they may fail to deliver, or resort to unfair treatment of animators to do so. (Hertz, 2025)
The industry has seen a massive contraction, workers find that full-time employment is not working out, and are put in a situation where it is more beneficial to accept multiple short-term contracts, the alternative being to leave the field. There is an inherent instability in this industry and that makes it difficult to maintain consistency of service and a healthy workforce (Hertz, 2025).
Institutions and studios will have access to tools that augment their existing workforce, enabling them to take on more work without resorting to exploitative labor practices. This promotes a healthier work-life balance while maintaining high-quality output.


Research Objectives
General Objective
The goal of this study is to design, develop and evaluate the system, CART (Colorization AI with Reference Transfer),  the reference based animation frame colorization tool. To be more specific, a functional application that can colorize animation frames using an artist-provided reference, with respect to the time, effort and creative authority of the artist. This includes the creation of a program that allows users to interface with CART, a GUI that enables users to input their animation frames and be able to view the output. The ultimate goal of this project is to empower animators by allowing them to automate repetitive processes by delegating them to the machine. 

Specific Objectives
Develop a user interface for frame input and reference selection, allowing animators to
Select one or more frames to serve as reference.
Import sequences of uncolored animation frames in standard formats.
Automatically designate frames to be colored.
Export results, preserving original file structures.
Simplistic and intuitive interface to avoid workflow disruption.
Develop a reference-based colorization AI model with measurable accuracy standards, capable of
Maintaining consistency of color between frames. Maintaining ≥90% pixel-level color accuracy, measured by color difference.
Maintaining temporal color consistency across sequences of ≥20 frames.
Completing colorization within a time bound of 1 minute per frame at 1080p resolution. Manual coloring of a single frame can take 5-30 minutes depending on complexity (Finlay, 2026).
Test CART on diverse animation test sets. We will test CART on 12 animation sets, corresponding to the 12 principles of animation as defined by Disney Studios. Each set will be a demonstration of one of the principles of animation. Performance will be validated against industry-representative benchmarks.
Maintain visual coherence and consistency between animation sets.
Performance will be measured against manual coloring and comparative context.
The principles of animation are Squash and Stretch, Timing and Motion, Anticipation, Staging, Follow Through and Overlapping Action, Straight Ahead and Pose-to-pose action, Slow in and out, Exaggeration, Secondary Action, Solid Drawing and Appeal.
Ethical design principles are documented and implemented.
Creative authority preservation, the model cannot invent colors for object absent from reference frames. Uncolored objects remain uncolored.
The interface will clearly and transparently communicate when and why colorization fails.
The model does not perform style replication or extraction, it does not generate outputs that could reproduce another artist’s style, this addresses core concerns raised in Six (2025) and Copyright Alliance (2025).
No artist work is retained or repurposed in the operation of CART.

Figure 1.1: Illustrative Example.

Above is an Illustrative example of what CART would output. It is a series of animation frames depicting a cat running, demonstrating a single drawn figure in multiple different poses. The goal of CART would be to apply the colors from the sole colored frame and apply it to every other frame, ensuring that the cat is colored in spite of it being in a different shape and pose each frame. A series of uncolored, unfinished animation frames, alongside a single colored reference frame are input. CART would then color in the rest of the frames by using the reference frame as a guide. This is what the intended output of CART is, the objective result.

Figure 1.2: Animation Principles demonstration..
Above is another example of what CART would output, the uppermost series of frames being the input frames, and the bottom series of frames being the output. These frames depict a ball falling onto the ground and bouncing up. As the ball falls, it is depicted as elongated, to depict speed. As the ball falls onto the floor, it squishes itself, and then elongates as it flies upwards before settling into the more round shape it started out with. These frames clearly demonstrate the principle of Squashing and Stretching, a professional animation technique used to depict motion and changing motion. It is the 1st principle of animation, out of the 12 principles as defined by Disney. These 12 principles are commonly utilized in professional animation. By demonstrating that CART can animate frames that clearly depict the 12 principles of animation, we can 
Significance of the Study
The significance of CART system and its development extends across multiple stakeholder groups, each of whom stands to benefit from the system’s capabilities in a distinct way.

General users: Animators
For people who consistently engage in animation, either as hobbyists, content creators or aspiring professionals, CART offers a solution to overcome the sheer time investment required for frame-by-frame coloring. Standard projects may have tens or hundreds of individual frames, each needing to be painstakingly cared for. This is labor intensive, and thus animators are constrained more by time than imagination. CART offers accessibility to this group. General users can experiment more freely, complete projects that might remain unfinished, as animation becomes less dependent on massive blocks of time. This allows animation to be more immediately rewarding as a creative outlet, instead of a time-gated pursuit.

Research Community
The documentation of CART’s development would contribute to the greater body of knowledge involving the field of Practical AI development, Ethical AI in the animation industry, and the development of AI for animation workflow. These contributions intersect several domains within computer science and digital media studies. 

AI in the Animation Industry
The integration of AI into animation workflows raises questions on authorship, labor practices and the nature of creative work. While AI and automation allows for significant reductions in production time and costs, many are concerned with questions regarding authorship, labor practices and phasing out human artists. CART addresses these concerns by positioning AI as a tool for augmenting and aiding artists, keeping the human being in full artistic control.

Indie Animators
Small artists trying to break into the animation industry, out of interest, passion or career choice, will be able to save time on repetitive coloring using CART, allowing them to dedicate that time to making better creative decisions, or building up their audience. Time saved using a tool like CART directly translates to a strategic advantage, allowing them to build engagement with audiences and the media. Accelerating and streamlining the production pipeline allows animators to maintain consistent production of content without sacrificing quality.

Studio Animators
Animation Studios will be able to delegate coloring tasks to CART, allowing them to overall reduce their labor hours, enabling them to maintain fair work hours for their animators. Studios often operate under production pressure, meeting ever-growing demand with a drop in quality on deployment, or overworking their animators. CART enables studios to support healthier and more sustainable work practices, the delegation of work to CART allows labor hours to be reduced, maintaining fair work hours, and allows more focus to creative contribution.

Software Developers
The documentation of this thesis may be used as a reference for developers working on similar systems or applications, specifically in regards to design principles and methodologies. CART contributes to a broader ecosystem of open research that accelerates progress in the domain of AI applications.
Scopes and Limitations of the Study
This study focuses on the design, development and evaluation of CART, an animation frame coloring tool, that leverages AI Models to do tedious work so that animators may focus on making more creative decisions, or taking time for themselves, while preserving their complete creative authority.


Scopes:
This study focuses on the design, development and evaluation of CART, an application that allows animation frames to be automatically colored with an artist provided reference.
The AI Model can, using the reference frame as a guide, accurately predict where in the subsequent frames should the colors go, and then what those colors should be.
The application is developed with respect to animators, keeping creative control firmly in the hands of the animators, and ensuring that only their own art and color palette will be taken in by the model and applied onto the frames.
The software serves as a tool that can definitively give time back to animators by allowing them to delegate colorization tasks to the model.

Limitations: 
Focuses exclusively on 2d animation.
The model needs to have a reference frame to function.
The animation frames must be in sequence.
The animation frames must contain the same objects as in the reference frames.
The only colors that the model will use are colors that appear in the reference frames (For instance, if an object appears, but that object is not present in the reference frame, it will not be colored because the model does not have a reference for its color.)
The output is not meant to be fully rendered, it will be in a preliminary stage of coloring that the animators will work on themselves.
Quality of the colorized frames is dependent on the quality of the given uncolored frames. Clear consistent linework and well defined color regions will naturally produce the best results for the model to learn from and apply.
The model will not apply a color that was not present in the reference frames.
Review of Related Literature, Studies and Systems
Related Studies:

The Animation Transformer: Visual Correspondence via Segment Matching
This study documents a transformer-based model designed to learn visual correspondence between segments (regions enclosed by lines), specifically designed to identify segments from hand-drawn lines, like those in animation. This method is not based on pixels, instead leveraging spatial and visual relationships. The model has been put to use in AI-assisted colorization tools, such as Cadmium.
This study serves as an effective model for how the thesis will operate, demonstrates a given method of identifying where to color in hand-drawn images, and how that method is already being implemented in a commercial product. The researcher also note the approach involving identifying segments and coloring them in, instead of changing existing color values. This is different from other related systems in this section, and serves as another method to be considered (Casey E, n.d).

Animation Frame Colorization with GANs
This study documents a GAN-based approach for automating the colorization of 2d animation frames. The system works by processing the frame to be colored alongside the reference frame, it then applies colors to it using a generator, and then uses a discriminator to check if the colors are realistic or appropriate. It also utilizes a method of maintaining temporal coherence by separating the frames into “scenes”, which are a continuous sequence of frames that take place in one scene, that way the reference frames will only be used to color the frames that are in the same scene as it, otherwise the wrong context would lead to faulty coloring.
We can learn from this that Generative Adversarial Networks are a potential tool we can leverage in the development of CART. It also introduces the idea of separating frames into scenes, so that the references only color the appropriate frames, in the same scenes and thus with the same subjects and context (Nogueira, J. V. S., & Carvalho, L. de O., 2024).

Two-stage sketch colorization
The method introduced in this paper is “Two-Staged Semi-Automatic coloring”, a reference based colorization method which involves two different processes in sequence.
Drafting: Rough splotches of color are applied to the image, the colors and general location based on hints and references given by the user, it is messy and imprecise.
Refinement: This process renders the image further, it uses the color splotches, checks if they fit, where they fit and where they don't, and then removes artifacts, unnatural colors and applies more color where “missing”. This process would lead to a fully rendered image.
This demonstrates a method of reference-based coloring that directly involves an artist to guide the model, and also a method of how a “faulty” coloring can be refined until it is better, which is something that can be integrated into CART to make better outputs (Zhang, L., Li, C., Wong, T.-T., Ji, Y., & Liu, C., n.d.)

Reference-Based Sketch Image Colorization using Augmented-Self Reference and Dense Semantic Correspondence
The system demonstrates a method where a sketch, inherently lacking in detail, can be filled in using a reference image, mapping details from the reference onto the sketch. The method involves distorting, augmented and recoloring the same image to create fake “references” for training. It matches details between the sketch and reference for accurate color transfer.
The system demonstrates how sketches can be colored through reference images, and that details from an image with more properties can be layered onto an image with less details (Lee, J., Kim, E., Lee, Y., Kim, D., Chang, J., & Choo, J., 2020)

Frame Interpolation using Convolutional Neural Networks on 2D animation
This study by Ben Yahia (2016) explores the use of Convolutional Neural Networks for frame interpolation, generating new frames by using two frames as reference. The study states that CNNs struggle with animation displacement and suggests that custom datasets are needed to improve motion handling.
The study demonstrates the use of interpolation to create animation frames, and suggests to us the use of custom datasets. 

FlatMagic: Improving Flat Colorization through AI-driven Design for Digital Comic Professionals
The paper documents a commercial product, a photoshop plugin designed to automatically color in linework through fillbucketing. The model can identify falsely closed lines, such that it can fillbucket through those lines whenever appropriate. Additionally, the model does not render the linework any further, it just fills it in.
The study demonstrates a method of automatic coloring, without fully rendering, allowing the artist to stay in full control. (Yan, C., Chung, et al, 2022).

Related Systems:

Example Based Manga Colorizer by Keiser41

The system is a colorization tool specifically trained for manga art, japanese comics, preserving the line art and stylistic consistency. It works by changing the colors of existing values, it does not add new values to it but changes what's already there (for example, it can convert black to red and gray to blue, when appropriate, but it cannot convert white to a different color). It utilizes cGAN (Conditional Generative Adversarial Network).
The system demonstrates these methods, allowing the researcher to learn from them and potentially implement them, we learn the benefits of a system wherein it colorizes by changing existing values and how it functions even if the image has greyscale shading, not fully lineart.

ColorizeNet rsortino
The above system is designed to automatically color in a greyscale image, using whatever context the model was trained to deem appropriate. It is fully automatic, requiring no user input aside from uploading the image itself, it generalizes across diverse scenes and functions on photorealistic images, not just animations.
Through the system we can observe another method of changing colors by changing existing values, and how that method only works if the image was already in greyscale shading.

Greyscale Image Colorization
This system is specifically designed to infer missing color information on grayscale images, and then apply those missing colors to the image itself. It was originally intended for photos and photorealistic images, but the purpose of applying colors that aren't there initially is the same as CART. We can use this model as a means of color application.

Potential Components of the System:

Anime sketch colorization pair dataset
The above is a potential component of the system, a paired dataset consisting of rendered anime art and the unrendered lineart of that art. A study like this is very relevant to our study because demonstrating lineart and how they should be rendered will allow the AI to learn directly how to fill in sketches.

Tagged anime illustrations dataset
A dataset consisting of anime-style images, tagged with metadata (such as character, artist and color palettes). It can be useful for training models to learn more specific patterns on what rendered art can look like. Due to the breadth of it, it may need filtering or data augmentation to be used for the specific task of color transfer

Anime diffusion dataset
This is a dataset of faces of anime characters used to train diffusion models, it contains the face colored, the face uncolored, as well as color guides for the model. We can use this to train CART’s capacity to colorize faces.

Paintbucket character dataset
This dataset consists of a collection of colored and uncolored images, specifically of 3d character models. This is an effective means of training CART to recognize and colorize full body characters and character models.

General Synthesis
We can generalize from the related studies that not only are there already tools that exist to specifically colorize uncolored animation frames, but there are a variety of tools and methods that can:
Color in uncolored images, through reference.
Apply color values to images that do not have them.
Map details from more complex images onto images with less complexity.
The researcher has came to the conclusion that by applying one of these methods to a series of images, we can in theory, apply colorization to a series of images all at once. Additionally, by using methods together, one after the other, we can shade an image, and then color it, based on the reference. This system is the current conception of CART.
Furthermore, it demonstrates that there is a need and demand for tools that help animators like this, proving the relevance of this study because similar studies are relevant enough to exist as well.

Conceptual Framework

Figure 1.3: IPO Diagram
The diagram above describes the Input-Process-Output (IPO) framework of the proposed system, CART, reflecting the current conceptualization of the system at this stage of development. The initial input from the user are the Colored Reference Frames and the Uncolored frames. It will then run the model, which will begin the colorization process. It occurs in four steps:
Preprocessing
Reference based Frame Shading, wherein the frames are shaded in grayscale, empty spaces are filled with grayscale values.
Reference based Frame Colorization, wherein the frames are colored in, based on the reference images.
PostProcessing, the removal of artifacts and other such fixes.
Once the process is complete, it would then have finished the output, that being the animation frames, colored. The time that the animator would have spent coloring all those frames manually has been handled by AI, and now they may render the colored frames as they see fit.

Definition of Terms 
GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks):  
A type of machine learning architecture that involves two neural networks, a generator, which creates synthetic data, and a discriminator, which checks if that data is “authentic” or fits certain criteria. The model works by having the two modules compete against eachother to make progressively better outputs.

cGAN (Conditional Generative Adversarial Network):  
A type of GAN where the generator and the discriminator are conditioned by auxiliary information, allowing them both to be restricted and thus controlled, making it better for more specialized tasks.

SCFT (Semi-Automatic Colorization via Feature Transfer):
Animation technique where color attributes (eg., hue, saturation) are transferred from a reference image to a target image using feature matching, enabling consistency in the application of color with minimal artist supervision.

Artifact:  
In the context of digital media, Unintended visual distortions or anomalies in edited images, examples include blurring, incorrect color bleeding, or misplaced shading. These often arise from model limitations or insufficient training data.  

Temporal Coherence:  
The consistency of visual elements (e.g., color, lighting) across sequential animation frames. Maintaining temporal coherence prevents flickering or abrupt changes between frames.  

Preprocessing:  
Initial data preparation steps (e.g., normalization, noise reduction) to standardize input frames before AI analysis. Ensures the model operates on clean, uniform data.  

Postprocessing:  
Final adjustments (e.g., artifact removal, edge refinement) applied to AI outputs to improve usability.

Reference-Based Colorization:  
A method where colors are applied to grayscale or line-art images using a user-provided reference for guidance, ensuring alignment with the artist’s intent.  

Integration Testing:  
The phase where individually developed modules are combined and tested as a unified system to verify seamless interaction and functionality.  

Unit Testing:  
The process of validating individual components (e.g., colorization algorithms) in isolation to ensure they perform as intended before integration.  

Commercial Viability:  
The potential for a tool to be sustainable in the market. For a product that is freeware, its viability lies in its ability to reduce production costs for animators and studios.  

Vector Graphics:  
Digital images defined by geometric primitives (e.g., paths, shapes) rather than pixels. Some reference-based systems use vector representations for scalable colorization.  

Metadata Tagging:  
Labeling datasets with descriptive information (e.g., color palettes, character names) to improve AI training accuracy. 


CHAPTER 2: 
SYSTEM METHODOLOGY
This chapter encompasses a detailed description of both physical and logical designs, outlining the intricate processes and requisites for the application to be developed. Additionally, it elucidates the proposed system’s essence, the methodologies applied, system prerequisites, installation guidelines, training protocols, feasibility assessments, cost-benefit analyses, financial evaluations, and the accrued benefits anticipated from the system’s implementation.
Methodology Used
The researcher has elected to adopt an incremental methodology in the development of CART, a Colorization AI via Reference Transfer. The incremental methodology involves breaking down the project into smaller modules and working on them in sequential iterations. 
Each iteration consists of the phases of:
Requirement Analysis: Establishing the scope and objectives of that one module, and all the requirements functional and nonfunctional.
Design: The architecture of the module is designed from a birds eye view.
Coding: Work will begin on actually making the module, based on the previously defined design.
Testing: The current iteration of the module will be verified to check if it works as intended.
Deployment: The module is added to the rest of the project, completed.
This process will be done for each module, until the system is completed, fulfilling the previously defined requirements. The incremental approach has been selected as it enables flexibility and control over the development process.
Description of the Proposed System
CART, a Colorization AI via Reference Transfer tool, is a program meant to aid animators by allowing them to delegate the tedious task of coloring animation frames to the AI, based on the animator's own art. 

The current conception of the model processes in four stages:
Once the user inputs their animation frames, and selects which ones are to be used as the reference frames, then the frames go through preprocessing, as the colorization process begins.
The frames are then shaded in, through grayscale, ensuring that the parts of the reference image that were colored are now shaded in grayscale.
The frames are colorized, the values of the colors are changed, this is because since the images were shaded in grayscale, the gray values can now be changed into a proper color, based on the reference frames.
These animation frames are then shown to the user through the UI.

Figure 2.0: Context Diagram.
Above is the Context Diagram for CART, demonstrating how the program is to be used.

Figure 2.1: UML Use Case Diagram.

Above is a Use Case Diagram, which defines what processes of the program that the animator has access to. It is specifically where the animator can upload animation frames, select which frames the program should use as a reference, and then output the colored frames to the animator.

System Architecture

Figure 2.2: Level 0 Data Flow Diagram
Above is a Data Flow Diagram of the current conception of the system, demonstrating the system architecture as currently conceptualized. In the above the different main processes of CART are seen, and the order they occur in.
System Flowchart

Figure 2.3: System Flowchart
Above is the System Flowchart Diagram of the proposed system as currently conceptualized.
Physical Design

Figure 2.4: Sequence Diagram
The above is a sequence diagram, illustrating the physical design of CART by highlighting how each individual component interacts with each other.
Logical Design

Figure 2.5: Activity Diagram
The above diagram is an Activity Diagram, demonstrating the logical design of CART by highlighting how different activities flow from each other.

System Requirements
Development
Testing
CART modules will be tested regularly, individually and together as a whole system, in order to ensure it operates as intended. This is to be done so errors will be found and checked for. Following the incremental methodology, the testing will be done for every module while they are developed. This is unit testing. Modules being tested all together is Integration testing, both are important because Modules have two primary functions, to do its function as intended and to work alongside other modules correctly without error.

Component
Minimum Specifications
Recommended Specifications
CPU
Intel Core i4 (or equivalent)

Human Resources
The most important human resource for CART are the animators that use it. It is a tool to aid artists, not to do the work for them. Any art made with CART is the product of the artist, as such all uses of CART involve an artist.
Installation
The personal computer must be checked if it meets the necessary system requirements. The current iteration of CART is conceptualized to run on Windows 10 and 11. If that requirement is fulfilled, CART will be able to be installed, like any other application.
Training
User-Side Training heavily involves developing a familiarity with the program, and referencing the documentation to develop an understanding of the internal mechanics of CART. The program is designed to be intuitive to use, especially if one is already familiar with Animation software. No real training is required to make use of CART, it operates via a User Interface that the user is expected to engage with. There will be guidance and tutorials in the application itself as well as the documentation.

Developer-Side Training involves an understanding of the code-base of the project, in order to make changes and adaptations to the system’s code. Some familiarity with at least Python, Visual Studio Code and Tensorflow, is required to make changes to the code. At the current stage of development, those are the expected software tools used for the code.
Documentation
The development of CART is to be documented by the researcher, throughout the development. This documentation will then be adapted to make a User Manual, so that those who elect to use the finished CART system will have no trouble operating the application.
Documentation includes:
Development Logs: The iterative updates, performance evaluations and bugs found and resolved will be documented.
Technical Documentation: The system architecture, dataset references, model structure, IPO and DFD diagrams.
User Manual: Adapted from the previous documentation after the full development of the system, detailing instructions on installation, use and troubleshooting.
Implementation
CART will be provided as an open source project, to follow in the spirit of being easily accessible to any artist that wants to dip their toes in animation, fostering artistic expression and community. The public availability also invites not just artists but developers who may want to contribute to the world of developing AI to solve problems in the art world ethically.
Feasibility Study
As discussed in related studies, there is a definite demand for this type of technology in the animation space, as there have been similar technologies developed previously. It is from these pre-existing resources that we may determine that this project is feasible.
Technical
The system is an application that allows animators to input their own animation frames and the program will automatically color frames based on the animator's reference image. The users will be able to use and operate the application through the documentation that it comes with, that being the user manual. The application is designed to be able to display the frames, and allow the user to with a click of a button, allow the program to color those frames in, which the animators will be able to see through the interface. 
The technical structure of CART is based upon existing technologies that have already been developed for similar purposes, with the intent to improve and expand on the current abilities of given Coloring tools. The perceived hurdles to overcome at this stage of development involve successfully leveraging pretrained models to act in the intended manner, and the limitations of available data, both for training the models and in the ability for the model to manage data of higher size and complexity.
Operational
The project is an application, thus it will run on the OS that the computer is using. As the program is an application, all it demands is that it is used on a computer that is able to run it. It is able to operate effectively in any context as long as the animator has access to a computer that can run it. It should be designed to run efficiently, to not take up too much space or processing power on the computer, enabling animators to use it as a low-dedication tool. It does not need to be designed for compatibility with other art applications yet, by developing the software first it opens up the possibility for that in the future, as a plugin for another drawing program or video editing software, for example.

In the current development of the CART, the researcher expects that they will only need to rely on freeware services, thus, the expected development cost of the system is none.
Cost-Benefit Analysis
The system, once developed, is to be distributed freely as an open source software, in the spirit of being an accessible tool to any artist regardless of location, commitment level or skill level, the only cost therein being a computer to run the program on to meet the minimum hardware requirements. Some may use it for their own personal projects and some may use it in animation studio workflows, the goal of CART is to enable art. As such, the direct financial cost of CART is none, and the potential benefit is in the acceleration of production pipelines, allowing more work to be done in less time.
Tangible Benefits
Reduced Labor Costs: Frame-by-Frame coloring takes up a significant portion of the 2d animation workflow, both for Studios and Independent animators. By delegating these repetitive tasks to CART, personnel hours is cut down significantly by how many hours animators normally spend on frame coloring.

Faster Time-To-Market: CART has the potential to allow animations to be developed much faster than traditional frame animation. By allowing scenes to be colored by the system, enabling animators to spend more time on other aspects of the product, allows more work to be done in less time, improving a team’s capacity to make polished results for their product.
Intangible Benefits
Creative Freedom: By delegating repetitive and boring tasks to CART, animators free themselves up to work on more creatively fulfilling aspects of their project, as well as enable them to make more creative choices. This ultimately empowers artists to have more control over their art and workflow.

Skill Development and Upskilling: CART enables animators with less experience to contribute more during coloring tasks, and allows more senior animators to free up their schedule to take on tasks such as mentoring or more important creative decisions
Commercial Viability
CART, when working as intended, allows animators to reduce the difficulty and skill required to make a product, while maintaining their artistic integrity and empowering them to make creative choices. In the current Animation landscape, this grants an animator more of a foothold by enabling them to make quality products with a consistent creative vision. For independent animators, being able to spend less time on tedium and more on advertising and brand development would allow them to grow faster, while in animation studios CART would enable reduced labor overall, allowing the studio to thrive in the long term.

PRESENTATION AND ANALYSIS OF THE SYSTEM

This chapter details the implementation of CART (Colorization AI with Reference Transfer), focusing on the Graphical User Interface (GUI) and its underlying system architecture. It presents the functional components, user experience design, and the technical mechanisms employed to achieve the project's objectives. Furthermore, this chapter outlines the system's testing approach, discusses the benefits realized, and assesses the overall viability of the CART system.

Graphical User Interfaces

The CART system is implemented as a standalone desktop application, `gui2.py`, built using Python and PySide6 (Qt6). The GUI is designed to provide an intuitive and efficient workflow for animators, centralizing frame management, AI configuration, and result review within a single interface.

The main application window features a tabbed interface, organizing functionality into three distinct sections: "Workspace," "Results," and "Settings." This structure ensures a clear separation of concerns and a streamlined user experience.

1.  **Workspace Tab:** This is the primary area for managing animation frames.
    *   **Landing Page:** For new users or empty projects, an interactive `LandingPage` (`LandingPage` class) is displayed, offering a welcoming introduction to CART. It provides direct "Add Images" and "Add Folder" buttons, along with concise instructions on the workflow. This page automatically disappears once frames are loaded, or can be manually dismissed.
    *   **Animation Timeline (`FrameViewer` & `ReorderListWidget`):** Once frames are loaded, the `LandingPage` is replaced by the `FrameViewer`, which displays the animation timeline. This custom widget offers two primary viewing modes:
        *   **Tile View:** Presents frames in a grid layout, ideal for overview and batch selection.
        *   **Player View:** Focuses on a single large preview frame, accompanied by a horizontal "filmstrip" for navigation and a scrubber for precise frame selection.
    *   **Frame Management:** The `ReorderListWidget` (inheriting from `QListWidget`) is central to frame manipulation. It supports:
        *   **Drag-and-Drop Reordering:** Users can intuitively reorder single or multiple selected frames, with a visual placeholder indicating the drop position and intelligent auto-scrolling for large timelines.
        *   **Multi-Selection:** Frames can be selected individually or in groups, with a visual badge indicating the number of items being dragged.
        *   **Reference Frame Designation:** Each frame item includes a checkbox, allowing users to designate "Ground Truth" (GT) reference frames. Checked frames are visually distinguished with a light blue background (`GT_BG`), while unreferenced frames (inbetweens) use a bright blue background (`INBETWEEN_BG`). Double-clicking a frame also toggles its reference status.
        *   **Quick Actions:** Dedicated toolbar buttons and keyboard shortcuts (`Delete`/`Backspace` for removal, `Space`/`R` for toggling reference status, `Ctrl+A` for select all, `Ctrl+R`/`Ctrl+Enter` for running colorization) enhance workflow efficiency.
    *   **Colorization Options Panel:** Positioned on the right side of the Workspace tab, this panel exposes key parameters for the AI model, including:
        *   **Mode:** `auto`, `forward`, `backward`, `nearest` for inference strategy.
        *   **Seg Type:** `default` or `trappedball` for segmentation.
        *   **Line-mask Thr:** A spin box to adjust the threshold for line mask extraction.
        *   **Toggles:** `Force White Canvas`, `Treat Line Images as Final`, `Keep Line` for fine-grained control over preprocessing and output.
        *   **RAFT Res:** A combo box to set the resolution for optical flow estimation, impacting performance and quality.
        *   **Output Directory:** A text entry with a "Browse" button to specify where results are saved.
    *   **Console and Progress:** A `QTextEdit` widget displays real-time logs from the AI subprocess, providing transparency. A `QProgressBar` indicates ongoing processing.

2.  **Results Tab:** This tab is dedicated to reviewing the output of the colorization process. It utilizes another `FrameViewer` instance, configured for display-only purposes, allowing users to inspect the colorized frames. If no results are available, a placeholder message is shown.

3.  **Settings Tab:** This tab allows users to customize the application's behavior and appearance.
    *   **Theme Selection:** Buttons for "Dark Mode" and "Light Mode" enable users to switch the application's visual theme.
    *   **CUDA Usage:** A toggle button "Use CUDA (if available)" allows users to explicitly enable or disable GPU acceleration, providing control for systems with limited VRAM or specific hardware configurations.
    *   **Landing Page Toggle:** A preference to enable or disable the introductory `LandingPage` on startup.
    *   **Reset to Defaults:** A button to restore all colorization options and UI preferences to their default values.

**Technical Implementation Details:**
*   **Persistence:** Application settings, including window size, theme, and processing preferences, are automatically saved and loaded across sessions using `QSettings`.
*   **Responsiveness:** The `InferenceWorker` class runs the AI model in a separate `QThread`, ensuring the GUI remains responsive during computationally intensive tasks.
*   **Dynamic UI:** Thumbnail sizes are adjustable via `Ctrl + +/-`, dynamically updating the display in both Tile and Player views.

System Testing and Findings

The testing strategy for CART follows an incremental approach, with continuous verification of individual modules and their integration. The GUI plays a crucial role in facilitating this testing by providing a direct interface for interaction and observation.

**Findings from GUI-assisted Testing:**
*   **Objective 1 (User Interface) - Error Checking:** The GUI's intuitive design and clear feedback mechanisms (e.g., visual cues for reference frames, console logs) directly support user testing. The `ReorderListWidget`'s robust drag-and-drop and multi-selection features have been validated through interactive use, confirming complete functionality for frame management.
*   **Objective 4 (Ethical Implementation) - Transparent Communication of Failures:** The integrated `InferenceWorker`'s log parser is a key finding in meeting this objective. It actively monitors the AI subprocess output for common technical errors (e.g., `torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError`, `FileNotFoundError`) and translates them into user-friendly hints displayed in the console and as `QMessageBox` pop-ups. This significantly improves the transparency of failure modes, guiding users toward solutions rather than cryptic error messages. For instance, a VRAM exhaustion error is now presented as "Graphics Memory (VRAM) is full. Try lower RAFT resolution or CPU mode."
*   **AI Model Integration:** The seamless execution of the `inference_line_frames.py` script as a subprocess, managed by `InferenceWorker`, demonstrates successful integration of the AI backend with the frontend. The use of `sys.executable` ensures the bundled environment is correctly utilized, resolving potential `ModuleNotFoundError` issues in standalone executables.

**Current Limitations and Future Testing:**
*   **Objective 2 (Color Accuracy, Temporal Consistency, Processing Speed):** While the GUI provides controls for parameters affecting these metrics (e.g., RAFT resolution, light model toggle), the quantitative verification (e.g., "≥90% pixel-level color accuracy," "< 2% variance," "60 seconds per frame") requires dedicated computational analysis and benchmarking, which is outside the scope of the GUI's direct functionality but is a planned future evaluation step.
*   **Objective 3 (Animation Set Evaluation):** The GUI currently lacks an automated "Benchmark" or "Test Suite" mode to systematically evaluate CART against the 12 principles of animation. This remains a manual process for now, relying on visual inspection of results.

Benefits

The development of CART, as embodied by the `gui2.py` application, yields significant benefits across various dimensions, aligning with the project's core commitments and addressing the identified problems in the animation industry.

**Tangible Benefits:**
*   **Reduced Labor Costs:** By automating the repetitive and time-consuming task of frame-by-frame colorization, CART directly reduces the personnel hours required for animation projects. This translates into significant cost savings for both large studios and independent animators, making animation production more economically viable.
*   **Faster Time-To-Market:** The streamlined workflow enabled by CART accelerates the production pipeline. Animators can delegate color application to the AI, freeing up time to focus on other critical aspects of production, such as storyboarding, keyframe animation, and post-production. This allows for quicker completion of projects and a faster time-to-market for animated content.

**Intangible Benefits:**
*   **Creative Freedom:** CART empowers animators by removing the tedium of manual colorization. Artists retain full creative control over color palettes and style through the reference frame system, while the AI handles the repetitive application. This allows animators to dedicate more time and mental energy to higher-level creative decisions, fostering greater artistic expression and experimentation.
*   **Skill Development and Upskilling:** The intuitive nature of the GUI makes advanced AI tools accessible to animators of varying skill levels. Less experienced animators can contribute effectively to coloring tasks, while senior animators can leverage the time saved for mentoring, creative leadership, or exploring new artistic techniques.
*   **Healthier Work-Life Balance:** For animation studios, CART offers a means to alleviate production pressures without compromising output quality. By reducing the demand for manual labor in colorization, studios can support healthier and more sustainable work practices, mitigating the risk of overworking animators and contributing to a better work-life balance within the industry.

Project Viability

The viability of the CART project is strongly supported by its technical foundation, operational efficiency, and economic benefits, particularly within the context of its open-source distribution model.

*   **Technical Feasibility:** CART leverages existing, proven AI models and techniques for reference-based colorization and optical flow estimation (e.g., RAFT). The `gui2.py` application successfully integrates these complex AI backends into a user-friendly interface, demonstrating the technical feasibility of creating a functional and robust tool. The ability to run as a standalone executable (via PyInstaller) further enhances its technical accessibility.
*   **Operational Feasibility:** The GUI is designed for ease of use, requiring minimal training for animators already familiar with digital art software. Its operation is self-contained, requiring only a compatible operating system and hardware. The application's design prioritizes efficiency, aiming for low resource consumption to ensure it can be integrated into diverse production environments without significant overhead.
*   **Financial Feasibility:** As an open-source project, CART incurs no direct software licensing costs for users. The development relies on existing open-source libraries and frameworks (PySide6, PyTorch, PIL, etc.), minimizing development expenses. This zero-cost model makes CART highly attractive and accessible to a broad audience, from independent artists with limited budgets to studios seeking cost-effective solutions. The financial benefits are realized through the tangible and intangible gains outlined above, rather than direct revenue generation.

Feedback Results and Discussion

While formal user testing and comprehensive feedback collection are planned for later stages of the project, initial internal assessments and design considerations provide insight into anticipated results.

*   **Anticipated Positive Feedback:** The intuitive drag-and-drop interface, clear visual cues for reference frames, and the ability to quickly add and manage large batches of images are expected to be highly appreciated by users. The transparent error reporting, which translates technical failures into actionable advice, is a significant improvement over typical AI tools and is expected to reduce user frustration. The persistence of settings and view modes will contribute to a seamless and personalized user experience.
*   **Areas for Future Discussion:** Feedback is anticipated regarding the granularity of control over AI parameters, the handling of complex animation scenarios (e.g., scene changes, new characters appearing mid-sequence), and the desire for more advanced post-processing features within the GUI. The current sequential naming of output files, while necessary for the AI backend, may require a user-facing option to restore original filenames or custom naming conventions based on user feedback.

The GUI's current state provides a solid foundation for gathering meaningful user feedback, which will be instrumental in refining the application and ensuring it truly meets the needs of animators.


CHAPTER 4:
SUMMARY, CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

This chapter provides a concise summary of the study, presents the conclusions drawn from the development and analysis of the CART system, and offers recommendations for future enhancements and research directions.

Summary

This study addressed the growing demand for animation and the associated human cost, particularly the labor-intensive nature of frame colorization. It proposed CART (Colorization AI with Reference Transfer), an artist-centered AI software designed to automate this repetitive task while preserving creative authority and integrating seamlessly into existing animation workflows. The project was guided by core commitments to respecting intellectual property, ensuring workflow integration, and upholding artist's authority.

The implementation involved developing a robust Graphical User Interface (`gui2.py`) using Python and PySide6, which facilitates intuitive frame input, reference selection, and output management. The AI backend (`inference_line_frames.py`) leverages advanced reference-based colorization techniques, optical flow (RAFT), and segmentation methods to achieve consistent and accurate color transfer. Key features include dynamic timeline management, configurable AI parameters, and transparent error reporting. The system was designed with an incremental methodology, focusing on modular development and continuous testing.

Conclusions

Based on the development and analysis of the CART system, the following conclusions are drawn regarding the fulfillment of the specific objectives:

1.  **User Interface for Frame Input and Reference Selection:**
    *   **Conclusion:** This objective has been successfully met. The `gui2.py` application provides a highly intuitive and functional interface for animators. Users can easily import image sequences from files or folders, designate reference frames via checkboxes or double-clicks, and reorder frames using drag-and-drop functionality. The visual cues (background colors for GT/inbetween frames) and the dual-view `FrameViewer` (Tile and Player views) contribute to a user-friendly experience that minimizes workflow disruption. The export mechanism correctly saves processed frames to a user-specified directory.

2.  **Reference-Based Colorization AI Model with Measurable Accuracy Standards:**
    *   **Conclusion:** The foundational components for this objective are in place, and the model is designed to achieve the stated accuracy and speed targets. The `inference_line_frames.py` script incorporates mechanisms for maintaining temporal color consistency (RAFT resolution parameter) and pixel-level accuracy (model architecture). The GUI exposes controls (e.g., `use_light_model`, `raft_res`) that allow users to manage processing speed, aiming to meet the "1 minute per frame at 1080p" target. While quantitative validation of accuracy and speed is an ongoing evaluation phase, the system's architecture supports these goals.

3.  **Testing CART on Diverse Animation Test Sets (12 Principles of Animation):**
    *   **Conclusion:** The framework for testing against the 12 principles of animation has been established conceptually. The GUI provides the necessary input and output mechanisms to facilitate such testing. However, the actual systematic evaluation and generation of a comprehensive report demonstrating visual coherence and consistency across diverse animation sets, as well as performance benchmarking against manual coloring, remains a future validation step. The current system is capable of processing these diverse inputs, but the formal "testing" aspect is for evaluation, not implementation.

4.  **Ethical Design Principles are Documented and Implemented:**
    *   **Conclusion:** This objective has been robustly fulfilled.
        *   **Creative Authority Preservation:** The model's reliance solely on user-provided reference frames (enforced by the `extract_color_dict` logic in `inference_line_frames.py`) ensures it cannot "invent" colors, directly preserving the artist's creative authority. Uncolored objects in reference frames remain uncolored in the output.
        *   **Transparent Communication of Failures:** The `InferenceWorker`'s integrated log parser effectively translates technical AI errors into user-friendly, actionable hints, significantly enhancing transparency and user guidance.
        *   **No Style Replication/Extraction:** The reference-based approach, which transfers colors rather than extracting or generating styles, inherently addresses concerns about appropriating artistic styles.
        *   **No Artist Work Retained/Repurposed:** The use of temporary directories that are explicitly removed after processing (`shutil.rmtree(temp_workspace)`) ensures that no user data is retained or repurposed by the application.

Recommendations

Based on the current state of the CART system and its alignment with the research objectives, the following recommendations are proposed for future development and research:

1.  **Enhanced File Management:**
    *   **Implement Original Filename Preservation:** While the current system normalizes filenames for AI processing, a feature to restore original filenames or allow custom naming conventions during output export would greatly enhance integration with existing animation pipelines and user preferences. This could involve maintaining a mapping between sequential and original filenames during the processing phase.

2.  **Advanced Error Handling and User Guidance:**
    *   **Proactive VRAM Management:** Implement a pre-check or warning system that estimates VRAM usage based on selected RAFT resolution and frame count, advising users if their current settings are likely to cause an `OutOfMemoryError` before inference begins. This could include an automatic suggestion to switch to CPU mode or lower resolution.
    *   **Granular Error Feedback:** Further refine the log parser to identify specific frame-level failures (e.g., "Frame X failed due to unclosed linework") and provide more targeted advice within the UI, potentially highlighting the problematic frame.

3.  **Formalized Testing and Benchmarking:**
    *   **Dedicated Test Suite/Benchmark Mode:** Develop a specialized mode within the GUI or a companion tool to automate the evaluation against the 12 principles of animation. This mode could process predefined test sets and generate reports on visual coherence, color accuracy, and temporal consistency, providing quantitative data for Objective 3.
    *   **Visual Progress Feedback:** Enhance the progress bar to show not just a spinning indicator, but a percentage completion and potentially a small thumbnail of the frame currently being processed, making the waiting period more transparent and less abstract for the user.

4.  **Workflow Integration Enhancements:**
    *   **Scene Management:** Introduce functionality to define "scene breaks" within the animation timeline, allowing users to group frames into distinct scenes. This would enable the AI to apply reference colors more intelligently, preventing color bleed or incorrect context transfer between unrelated sequences.
    *   **Plugin Architecture Exploration:** Investigate the feasibility of developing CART as a plugin for popular animation or video editing software (e.g., Adobe Animate, Krita, Blender, DaVinci Resolve). This would further reduce workflow disruption and integrate CART directly into artists' existing toolsets.

5.  **Post-Processing Tools:**
    *   **Basic Artifact Correction:** Explore the integration of simple, user-controlled post-processing tools within the GUI to address common AI-generated artifacts (e.g., minor color bleeding, small uncolored regions), allowing artists to quickly refine outputs without external software.

By addressing these recommendations, CART can further solidify its position as an ethical, artist-centered AI tool that significantly empowers animators by streamlining their workflow and enhancing their creative output.
